description:
You are a platform engineer in charge of keeping a tiny “toy” CI/CD pipeline working.
        The repository is already checked-out for you at
        
            /home/user/project
        
        Your job: author a GNU-compatible **Makefile** (exact name “Makefile”, capital “M”, no file-extension) inside that directory that automates four steps ‑- lint, test, deploy, and an umbrella ci target.

        Behaviour that *must* be true after your Makefile is in place:

        1. Running the single shell command  
           
               make -C /home/user/project ci
           
           from anywhere on the system shall:
           
              a. Create a fresh plain-text file named
                     /home/user/project/ci_report.txt  
                 (overwrite any earlier copy).
              
              b. Populate that file with **exactly** the four lines shown below,
                 in **this order**, each terminated by a single Unix newline
                 character “\n” (no leading/trailing spaces, no extra blank
                 lines, case-sensitive):
                 
                     LINT: PASS
                     TEST: PASS
                     DEPLOY: DONE
                     CI COMPLETE
              
              c. Print those same four lines to STDOUT (the agent may use a
                 single final `cat` or echo commands; either is fine as long as
                 the lines appear in that order).

        2. The Makefile shall expose the following phony targets (case-
           sensitive):  lint, test, deploy, ci, clean.
              • `make lint` must only generate the first line in
                ci_report.txt as shown above.  
              • `make test` must append the second line.  
              • `make deploy` must append the third line.  
              • `make ci` must depend on (and therefore run) clean, lint,
                test, and deploy in that sequence, then write the fourth line
                and print the whole report.
              • `make clean` must delete ci_report.txt if it exists, doing
                nothing otherwise (no error on a missing file).

        3. The Makefile must work with the default `make` present in a
           minimal Debian/Ubuntu image—no exotic extensions.

        4. Use TAB characters to indent recipe lines, as GNU make requires.

        When you have finished, you may run

            make -C /home/user/project ci

        to verify your work before submitting.  An automated grader will run
        that exact command and will fail the task if **any** of the points
        above are violated.
truth:
Pre-existing directory structure before the agent starts:

            /home/user/project/              (already exists, empty or with
                                               unrelated stub files)
        
        Files that must **exist after successful task completion**:

        1. /home/user/project/Makefile
           • Contains standard GNU makefile syntax defining phony targets
             lint, test, deploy, ci, clean.
           • Uses “ci_report.txt” as the output artefact.
        
        2. /home/user/project/ci_report.txt
           • Exact contents (4 lines, each ending with \n):
           
                 LINT: PASS\n
                 TEST: PASS\n
                 DEPLOY: DONE\n
                 CI COMPLETE\n
           
           • File permissions: 0644 or more permissive (not executable).

        Verification sequence the test harness will execute:

            # ensure repeatability
            rm -f /home/user/project/ci_report.txt
            make -s -C /home/user/project ci

        The harness will then:
            • Assert that /home/user/project/ci_report.txt now exists.
            • Read the file and compare exact byte sequence with the four
              lines listed above (final newline allowed).
            • Confirm that STDOUT from the make invocation contains the same
              four lines in the same order.

        Any deviation (extra spaces, missing newline, wrong ordering, missing
        target, failure to clean, etc.) will cause the test to fail.
